segments.title: Mapping the opening encounter at the River Plate
segments.summary: A battle map introduces the opposing movements at first contact and centers the overview on HMS Exeter's part in the action.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses a broad track chart to orient the opening of the Battle of the River Plate. He notes that the overview gives particular attention to HMS Exeter as Admiral Graf Spee and the British force sight one another around 6 a.m. and begin maneuvering.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces the action map, its emphasis on Exeter, and the forces sighting one another around 6 a.m.

segments.title: Graf Spee opens fire at long range
segments.summary: Graf Spee's rapid decision to fire near maximum range is presented as a revealing sign of tactical weakness rather than confidence.
segments.body: The engagement develops quickly after contact, with Admiral Graf Spee opening fire at about 6:15. Dr. Clarke interprets firing at the longest available range as an admission of weakness: the German ship is trying to exploit its reach before the British cruisers can close and improve their own chances of hitting.

evidence.note: The transcript dates Graf Spee's opening fire to about 6:15 and characterizes the long-range choice as an admission of weakness.

segments.title: British cruisers reach their intended attack position
segments.summary: Exeter and Ajax nearly straddle Graf Spee after reaching a favorable position, but Exeter has already absorbed damaging hits as the German ship's main target.
segments.body: By about 6:50, the British cruisers have maneuvered into the position they sought and their gunfire is beginning to bracket Admiral Graf Spee. The advantage comes at a cost: HMS Exeter has been treated as the principal target and has already taken serious punishment. The rough battle map emphasizes the geometry of the approach rather than claiming exact ship tracks.

evidence.note: The transcript says Exeter and Ajax are nearly straddling Graf Spee from their desired position while Exeter has already taken heavy damage as the main target.

segments.title: Langsdorff and Harwood fight for different victories
segments.summary: The tactical choices become clearer when Langsdorff's need to preserve a commerce raider is contrasted with Harwood's pursuit of Graf Spee's destruction.
segments.body: Admiral Graf Spee enters a kind of fight unlike its earlier attacks on undefended merchant shipping, facing smaller warships that fire back aggressively. Dr. Clarke argues that Captain Hans Langsdorff and Commodore Henry Harwood therefore measure victory differently. Langsdorff can succeed by surviving, remaining operational, and escaping to resume commerce raiding; Harwood seeks the German ship's annihilation.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts Graf Spee's earlier easy actions with the British attack and explicitly distinguishes Langsdorff's survival goal from Harwood's annihilation goal.
